definition: Ignorant wanting directed toward ending, annihilating, or escaping a mode of becoming.
notes: The project keeps ignorant wanting for non-becoming as the default so the compound inherits the house rule for taṇhā while staying explicit about vibhava. Craving for annihilation and craving for non-becoming remain controlled alternates when explanatory prose needs stronger continuity with familiar Buddhist-English terminology. This term should stay coordinated with kāmataṇhā and bhavataṇhā as one member of the standard threefold taṇhā family. It also stays distinct from nirodha and virāga: the term names wanting to end, erase, or get out of becoming, not the actual quenching or fading of the problem. The OSF glossary supports keeping the craving family explicit rather than letting non-becoming language masquerade as release.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose emphasizing the annihilating impulse
context_rules.notes: Use this as a controlled alternate.

context_rules.context: In prose where continuity with bhava-language matters more than maximal explicitness
context_rules.notes: Use this as a controlled alternate.

context_rules.context: When translating the classic threefold taṇhā list alongside kāmataṇhā and bhavataṇhā
context_rules.notes: Keep the family wording aligned across the whole set.

example_phrases.notes: One of the classic threefold forms of craving.

authority_basis.scope: Named in notes as support for the current vibhavataṇhā policy.

translation_policy.default_scope: Use 'ignorant wanting for non-becoming' as the default rendering in the entry's governed translation contexts unless a recorded context rule overrides it.
translation_policy.when_not_to_apply: Yield to recorded context_rules, source-facing technical use, or other explicitly documented local reasons rather than rotating among alternates ad hoc.
translation_policy.drift_risk: Prevents drift back toward 'death wish' or other unrecorded alternates.
